# Introduction: The Defender's Mindset {.unnumbered}

> *An attacker has to find one way in. A defender has to think about all of them. This book is about learning to think like both.*

## The unlocked door behind the badge

Every organisation says its systems are secure. It puts a padlock icon on the login page, a
privacy policy in the footer, and an "enterprise-grade encryption" line on the marketing
site. Then, somewhere behind all of that, there is a server no one has patched in eleven
months, a password four people share, and a staff member about to click a link they
shouldn't. The claim and the reality are two different things — and the gap between them is
where information security lives.

This book is a first course in closing that gap. It will not turn you into a hacker or a
penetration tester in twelve weeks. It will do something more useful: teach you to *think
like a defender* — to look at a system and see, before anything goes wrong, where it could,
what it would cost, and what to do about it. That habit of mind is the thing you take into
any job, in any industry, for the rest of your career.

## How to think like a defender

Defenders reason in a particular way, and the whole book is built to grow that habit:

- **Assume nothing is safe until it is shown to be.** A control that looks fine on paper is a
  claim, not a fact, until you have reason to trust it.
- **Understand the attacker to protect against them.** You cannot defend what you do not
  understand, so we spend real time on how attacks actually work — not to do them, but to see
  what they need and take it away.
- **Weigh the cost.** Perfect security is unaffordable and unusable. Every control is a
  trade-off, and a defender's real skill is deciding which ones are worth it.
- **Never forget the human.** Most breaches do not defeat the technology; they walk past it
  through a person. The human factor is not a footnote — it is often the whole story.

## How this book works

The book runs alongside the unit, **one chapter per teaching week**. Read the chapter *before*
that week's lecture. The chapter carries the knowledge — the definitions, the concepts, the
worked ideas — so that the lecture does not have to. That frees the two hours in the room for
the things a page cannot do: watching an attack run live, arguing a judgment call, and testing
where the whole room actually disagrees. Then the lab puts your hands on the tools.

So the rhythm each week is: **read → discuss and see → do.** Each chapter ends with a few
**Before the lecture** questions. They are not graded; they exist to prime your thinking and
they are usually where the lecture's opening poll begins. Come with a view, even a wrong one —
being wrong out loud and finding out why is most of how this subject is learned.

## A word on using AI

You are encouraged to use AI freely in this unit, including while you read this book. Ask it
to explain a concept a second way, to quiz you, to challenge your answer to a Before-the-
lecture question. What matters is that you stay in the conversation and do the thinking — use
it to understand faster, not to skip understanding. The unit's companion guide,
[*Conversation, Not Delegation*](https://michael-borck.github.io/conversation-not-delegation/),
is about exactly this skill, and the unit's AI policy sets out where AI is encouraged and
where you work unaided.

---

*This book is open access under a Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) licence. It is part of
the [books.borck.education](https://books.borck.education) series.*
